plainMeaning: Whatever wrong I have bound in my mind, spoken with my words, or done with my body — may all of it be undone.
recitation: Jaṁ jaṁ maṇeṇa baddhaṁ, jaṁ jaṁ vāyāe bhāsiyaṁ pāvaṁ; kāeṇa vi dukkayaṁ, micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ tassa.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit relative pronoun jaṁ < Sanskrit yad (neuter), correlated with tassa (tad) later in the line.
wordByWord.notes: Neuter singular agreeing with pāvaṁ / dukkayaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Reduplication of jaṁ < yad; the doubling is distributive, sweeping in every instance without exception.
wordByWord.notes: The repetition is intentional, not scribal — it stresses totality.

wordByWord.etymology: Instrumental singular of maṇa < Sanskrit manas ('mind'); Prakrit -eṇa = Sanskrit instrumental -ena.
wordByWord.notes: First of the three instruments (trikaraṇa): mind, speech, body.

wordByWord.etymology: Past passive participle baddha < √bandh ('to bind'); cognate with Sanskrit baddha.
wordByWord.notes: The verb chosen for the mind is 'bound' — fault begun in thought is literally karma fastened to the soul.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit relative pronoun jaṁ < Sanskrit yad, opening the second (speech) clause.
Reduplication of jaṁ < yad; parallels the doubling in the first clause.
Instrumental singular of vāyā < Sanskrit vāc / vākyā ('speech'); Prakrit -āe = feminine instrumental.
Past passive participle bhāsiya < √bhāṣ ('to speak'); = Sanskrit bhāṣita.
Prakrit pāva < Sanskrit pāpa ('sin, evil'); neuter singular, the object governing both jaṁ clauses.
wordByWord.notes: Stated once but understood across the mind- and speech-clauses alike.

wordByWord.etymology: Sandhi of kāeṇa + vi. kāeṇa = instrumental of kāya ('body') < Sanskrit kāya; vi = Prakrit emphatic particle < Sanskrit api ('also, even, too').
wordByWord.notes: Two tokens written together: the third instrument (body) plus 'too', drawing the triad to a close.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dukkaya < Sanskrit duṣkṛta ('badly done, misdeed'), from dus- ('ill') + kṛta ('done'). Distinct from duḥkha ('pain').
wordByWord.notes: Verb matched to the body: sin 'done'. Note the deliberate verb-gradation baddhaṁ → bhāsiyaṁ → dukkayaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit micchā < Sanskrit mithyā ('falsely, vainly'); here adverbial — 'may it be made null'.
Prakrit enclitic mi < Sanskrit me ('my, by me'), first-person genitive/instrumental.
wordByWord.notes: Personalizes the retraction: the misdeed is owned as the speaker's own.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dukkaḍa < Sanskrit duṣkṛta ('misdeed'); the same root as dukkayaṁ above, here as the noun in the fixed formula.
wordByWord.notes: micchā + mi + dukkaḍaṁ = the standard retraction-formula = Sanskrit mithyā me duṣkṛtam.

wordByWord.etymology: Genitive singular of the demonstrative ta < Sanskrit tasya ('of that'); correlative to the opening jaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Singular 'of that' gathers every fault of mind, speech and body into one object of renunciation.

literalTranslation: Whatever sin has been bound by the mind, whatever sin has been spoken by speech, and whatever has been ill-done by the body too — of that, my misdeed, may it be void.
idiomaticTranslation: Whatever evil I have fastened in my mind, whatever evil my tongue has uttered, and whatever my body has wrongly done — for all of it I withdraw my deed: may my misdeed come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The verse opens the recitation by funnelling every conceivable fault through the three instruments of action — mind, speech, body (trikaraṇa) — and then disowning all of them in a single breath. Its artistry lies in the verb chosen for each instrument. The mind's sin is baddhaṁ, 'bound': a fault conceived in thought is already karma fastened to the soul, even before a word or gesture follows. Speech's sin is bhāsiyaṁ, 'spoken': it escapes into the world as utterance. The body's sin is dukkayaṁ, 'ill-done': it is enacted, completed. Reading the three verbs in order — bound, spoken, done — traces a fault's whole career from inner intention to outward act, so that nothing in the chain escapes confession. The doubled jaṁ jaṁ ('whatever, whatever') refuses every loophole; pāvaṁ ('sin') is named only once but governs both the mind- and speech-clauses, while vi ('too') in kāeṇa vi pointedly adds the body so the triad is sealed. The line then resolves into the tradition's retraction-formula, micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ — 'may my misdeed be void' — with the singular tassa ('of that') collecting the scattered faults into one object that is set aside at once. The first-person mi keeps the act personal: this is not a general lament but the reciter owning and annulling his own deeds.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain teaching that karmic bondage (bandha) arises through yoga — the activity of mind, speech and body — driven by the passions. By confessing along exactly these three channels the reciter performs ālocanā (disclosure) and then nindā/garhā (self-censure and renunciation): the heart of the fourth Āvaśyaka. The formula micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ (Sanskrit mithyā me duṣkṛtam, 'may my misdeed become null') does not magically erase karma; it expresses saṁvara — the resolve that the fault, disowned, will bear no further fruit and will not be repeated. The pairing of baddhaṁ with the mind is doctrinally precise: Jainism holds that even unexpressed mental wrong binds karma, so the confession must begin there.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As the reciter winds up the equanimity-vow, this verse is a quick, total self-audit: pause and ask 'what did I think, say, and do wrongly?' — then let each go with micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. Said sincerely rather than by rote, it carries the balance just practised out into daily speech and conduct.

crossReferences.text: Iriyāvahiyaṁ — the movement-confession that also closes on micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ
crossReferences.source: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipakṣa ed.)

crossReferences.text: Tassa-uttari (purification of self through self-censure, nindā and garhā by kāyotsarga)

sources.title: Micchami Dukkadam
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit→Sanskrit equivalence micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ = mithyā me duṣkṛtam and its 'may my misdeed be void' sense.

sources.title: Pratikramana
sources.consultedFor: The threefold (mind/speech/body) confession framework and the role of retraction within pratikramaṇa.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Hargovind Das T. Sheth), Prakrit-Hindi dictionary (scan)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit roots baddha (√bandh), bhāsiya (bhāṣita), dukkaya/dukkaḍa (duṣkṛta), and instrumental forms maṇeṇa/vāyāe/kāeṇa.

sources.title: ISJS Online Prakrit Dictionary
sources.consultedFor: Cross-check of pāva (pāpa), vi (api), and the relative/demonstrative jaṁ…tassa (yad…tasya) correlation.

notes: This is a widely transmitted Prakrit gāthā; the Anchalgachchha book's verbatim reading writes કાએણવિ (kāeṇa + vi) as a single sandhi-unit and pāvaṁ once though it governs both relative clauses — both preserved here, not normalized. dukkayaṁ here is duṣkṛta ('ill-done'), NOT duḥkha ('pain'); some online glossaries conflate them. Variant recensions read 'kāeṇa kammuṇaṁ' or add 'tassa micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' differently; this gloss follows the book's wording. Cited URLs are general/encyclopedic and dictionary-level rather than a critical edition of this exact gāthā; treat fine points of recension as approximate.
